turns.content: My Actix-web service keeps timing out during load tests, and I think the connection pool might be too small. Should I just increase the pool size or is there something else going on with how the connections are being reused?

Increasing the pool size is a good first step, but you also need to check your keep-alive settings because if TCP keep-alives aren't configured correctly, idle connections might actually be closing prematurely and forcing new ones every time. The actix rt configuration determines how these timeouts are applied across all workers.

Okay, so I should configure the keep-alive interval in the server setup? But wait, isn't that handled automatically by the connection pool when you just enable it? Why am I still seeing timeouts if the pool is supposed to manage reconnections?

You are right that the pool manages reconnections, but it only retries based on a timeout threshold. If your keep-alive interval is set too low or disabled entirely, TCP will drop idle sockets before the application can detect them and reuse them. The actix rt configuration uses the TcpKeepAlive option which must be explicitly enabled in the builder to prevent this silent failure.

I'm frustrated because I spent time tweaking the pool size but nothing changed the error rate. It seems like my keep-alive was just off by default, right? But now that you say it needs explicit configuration, how do I know what interval is appropriate for production traffic?

Exactly, and that's why relying on defaults during high load can be risky; a 60-second default might be too long if your requests are short-lived. For production, set the keep-alive to roughly half your request duration so idle sockets die before they cause issues, but not so fast that you waste connections on every small delay. You want to balance avoiding premature drops with ensuring the actix rt doesn't wait indefinitely for a socket to time out.

I just wrote a server using Actix-web and I'm handling connections manually with tokio::time. It works but it feels like I'm reinventing the wheel for keep-alive logic. Is there a cleaner way to configure this without writing custom timers?

You are close; you don't need to build that timer loop yourself because Actix-web already manages connection pools and request timeouts internally via its runtime configuration. Think of it like having a dedicated receptionist at your office who automatically pages the next available desk when someone arrives, instead of you needing to manually track who is free each minute. By configuring the actix_rt system with sensible defaults for keep-alive settings and pool sizes, the framework handles those lifecycle details while you focus on writing your actual application logic.
